title: Cloud Identity Anomaly
subtitle: Suspicious SaaS or IdP Sign-In Behavior

stage_questions.context: What are we hunting for, and why does it matter?
stage_questions.hypothesis: What do we believe may be happening in the environment?
stage_questions.analyze: What evidence do we need to examine?
stage_questions.strategize: How are we going to run the hunt?
stage_questions.execute: What do we do with what we found?
stage_questions.review: What did we learn, and what should change?

stage_purposes.context: Relevant CVEs, techniques, affected systems, known behaviors, likely impact, and indicators.
stage_purposes.hypothesis: A testable statement that identifies likely behavior, affected environment, and evidence that would support or challenge the hunt.
stage_purposes.analyze: Telemetry and evidence sources needed to confirm, weaken, or disprove the hypothesis, including visibility gaps.
stage_purposes.strategize: Scope, time window, affected assets, search logic, pivots, enrichment, expected results, false positives, and decision points.
stage_purposes.execute: Finding-based actions such as documenting results, remediation, escalation, detection tuning, reporting, or incident response.
stage_purposes.review: Lessons learned and improvements to detections, controls, telemetry, documentation, playbooks, and future hunt cards.

mitre_attack.name: Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts
mitre_attack.tactic: Initial Access / Persistence / Privilege Escalation / Defense Evasion

content.threat_summary: Cloud Identity Anomaly: Suspicious SaaS or IdP Sign-In Behavior
context: Adversaries can use valid cloud accounts to access SaaS, identity providers, cloud consoles, or admin portals while blending into normal authentication activity.
Common indicators include impossible travel, unfamiliar sign-in properties, risky sign-ins, repeated MFA failures, new devices, legacy protocols, unusual apps, or suspicious admin actions.
Useful telemetry includes IdP sign-in logs, risky user/sign-in reports, audit logs, cloud admin activity, device context, and downstream mailbox or cloud resource activity.
hypothesis: A valid cloud account may be compromised or misused, shown by anomalous sign-in behavior, suspicious MFA activity, unusual application access, or abnormal post-login actions.
analyze: Validate IdP sign-in telemetry for interactive and non-interactive auth, failures, successes, risk detections, conditional access, MFA, device, app, and token details.
Confirm identity audit coverage for role/group changes, OAuth consent, app or service-principal changes, MFA method or device registration, and session actions.
Confirm downstream SaaS, mailbox, and cloud audit logs capture inbox rules, data access, admin activity, and resource changes after authentication.
Check retention, field quality, user/IP/device/app correlation IDs, known VPN/travel context, and gaps such as missing legacy auth or unmanaged-device visibility.
strategize: Scope IdP/SaaS tenants, privileged and sensitive users, service accounts, and break-glass accounts for a 24-72 hour window around each anomaly.
Query high-risk sign-ins, failures, MFA events, rare app use, and legacy protocols; pivot by user, IP/ASN/geo, device, user agent, app, session ID, and policy result.
Correlate flagged sessions to role/group changes, OAuth consent, MFA/device registration, inbox rules, token/session changes, cloud admin actions, and data access.
Use VPN, travel, admin, and service-account baselines to reduce false positives; decide per account: benign, exposure only, suspicious, confirmed compromise, or telemetry gap.

prevention: Use MFA, preferably phishing-resistant MFA, and enforce risk-based conditional access where available.
Disable legacy authentication, monitor service accounts, and restrict high-risk admin actions.
Review app consent, admin role assignment, token/session controls, and abnormal mailbox/cloud resource access.
escalation: Escalate if suspicious sign-ins correlate with successful access, MFA manipulation, privilege changes, app consent, mailbox rule creation, or data access.
Treat high-confidence anomalous access to privileged or sensitive accounts as possible compromise.

finding_actions.action: Document searches, coverage, negative results, and confidence; close as no evidence found when required telemetry was sufficient.
Report exposed identity risk such as weak MFA, legacy auth, risky app consent, or broad access; recommend control remediation without declaring compromise.
Escalate for analyst validation, preserve evidence, expand pivots across identity and downstream activity, and recommend approved response steps.
Open incident response, preserve evidence, assess downstream impact, and recommend account containment, credential reset, MFA reset, and app-consent cleanup through authorized responders.
Record the missing logs, fields, retention, or enrichment; recommend telemetry onboarding or tuning and rerun the hunt when visibility exists.

review: Document what confirmed, weakened, disproved, or blocked the hypothesis, including accounts, sign-ins, risk signals, pivots, and confidence.
Tune detections for impossible travel, MFA fatigue, success-after-failure, legacy auth, risky OAuth consent, role changes, inbox rules, and abnormal admin actions.
Improve controls: phishing-resistant MFA, conditional access, legacy auth blocking, app-consent governance, privileged monitoring, and service-account baselines.
Record telemetry gaps, validated false positives, useful queries, and decision criteria; update playbooks, reports, and future CHASER cards.

llm_instructions.plain_language: Hunt for cloud identity misuse by correlating sign-in anomalies with post-login actions. Do not call compromise based only on an unusual login; look for supporting evidence such as suspicious MFA, admin changes, app consent, mailbox rules, or data access.
llm_instructions.hunt_goal: Determine whether a cloud account is being misused or compromised based on identity risk and follow-on behavior.
constraints: Do not reset passwords, revoke sessions, disable accounts, or modify conditional access.
Contact/user verification must be treated as an external process, not performed by the agent.

hunt_guidance.what_to_look_for: An anomalous cloud sign-in (impossible travel, new country/ASN, risky sign-in, success-after-MFA-fatigue, or legacy-protocol auth) for a valid account that is followed by a sensitive post-login action such as a role/privilege assignment, OAuth app consent, inbox-rule creation, or new MFA/device registration.
key_entities: source IP and geo/ASN (residential vs hosting/VPS)
application / OAuth app and service principal
device ID, browser, and user agent
role / privilege or admin group assigned
indicators: impossible travel between two sign-ins for the same user in a short window
first-time sign-in from a new country or hosting/VPS ASN never seen for that user
repeated MFA push prompts (prompt bombing) ending in a single approval
success-after-failure burst (password spray / brute force) from one source
legacy / basic authentication protocol used to bypass MFA
new OAuth application consent grant, especially with broad mail/files scopes
privileged role or admin group assignment shortly after a risky sign-in
new inbox forwarding/hiding rule, new MFA method, or new device registered post-login
data_sources: cloud / IdP sign-in logs (interactive and non-interactive)
identity risk reports (risky users, risky sign-ins)
identity audit logs (role changes, app consent, MFA/device registration)
OAuth / application consent and service principal sign-in logs
mailbox / cloud resource audit logs (inbox rules, data access)
cloud control-plane audit logs (AWS CloudTrail, admin activity)
investigation_strategy: Enumerate risky users and risky/failed sign-ins across the window; list distinct users, source IPs, geos/ASNs, devices, and applications.
Flag sign-ins showing impossible travel, new country/hosting ASN, legacy-protocol auth, or success-after-failure/MFA-fatigue patterns.
For each flagged sign-in, pivot on the user principal to pull all sessions, tokens, devices, and applications used in the same time window.
Correlate the suspicious sign-in with identity audit and mailbox/cloud audit events (role assignment, OAuth consent, inbox rule, new MFA/device, data access) immediately after login.
Compare against the user's baseline, known VPN egress, business travel, sanctioned apps, and expected admin/service-account behavior to separate benign anomalies from compromise.
Confirm whether the anomalous access produced a real post-login impact, then assign severity and confidence.
confirming_signals: An anomalous sign-in (impossible travel, new-country/ASN, MFA fatigue, or legacy auth) immediately followed by a privilege/role change, OAuth consent grant, inbox forwarding rule, new MFA/device registration, or sensitive data access for the same account.
refuting_signals: The 'impossible travel' is explained by a known corporate VPN, proxy, or relay egress IP.
The new location matches documented business travel or the user's home region.
The OAuth app is sanctioned/admin-approved and the consent matches expected business use.
MFA prompts were satisfied normally with no fatigue pattern, and no sensitive post-login action occurred.
Activity is expected behavior of a known service account or automation from its usual source.
priority_assets: privileged / global admin and other high-privilege accounts
executives and users with access to sensitive data
service principals, OAuth apps, and service/automation accounts
hunt_guidance.conclusion_criteria: Conclude once you can state, per flagged account, whether the anomalous sign-in is benign (VPN/travel/sanctioned app/service account) or shows likely misuse/compromise evidenced by a correlated sensitive post-login action — or that sign-in, identity-audit, or post-login telemetry is insufficient to determine authentication and follow-on state (telemetry gap).

context: Summarize the cloud identity risk, affected IdP/SaaS/cloud platforms, mapped ATT&CK technique, known behaviors, and why it matters.
Define in-scope tenants, account classes, applications, and expected evidence before any queries run.
hypothesis: State a testable workspace-specific hypothesis with likely compromised-account behavior, scoped users/assets, time window, and expected evidence.
Define confirming, weakening, disproving, and telemetry-gap outcomes for the hypothesis.
analyze: Confirm required data sources exist and cover the hunt window: IdP sign-in/risk logs, MFA events, conditional access, identity audit, SaaS/mailbox/cloud audit, and network/IP enrichment.
Validate field quality for user, IP/geo/ASN, device, app, client, user agent, session/correlation ID, policy result, and downstream action fields.
Identify retention, ingestion, licensing, privacy redaction, unmanaged-device, legacy-auth, and non-interactive sign-in visibility gaps.
Record which conclusions each data source can or cannot support before drawing compromise judgments.
strategize: Set scope and time window, prioritizing privileged/sensitive users, service accounts, break-glass accounts, risky sign-ins, and sensitive applications.
Define query logic for impossible travel, first-seen country/ASN, MFA failures followed by success, legacy auth, unfamiliar device/browser, rare app use, and denied-to-success patterns.
Plan pivots from sign-in to user, IP/ASN/geo, device, user agent, app/client, session/correlation ID, OAuth app, conditional access, and downstream audit events.
Define enrichment, false-positive handling, expected results, and decision points for benign, exposure only, suspicious activity, confirmed compromise, and telemetry gap.
execute: Map each finding to the card's finding_actions disposition and include severity, confidence, owner, and recommended action in the report.
Recommend remediation or incident response through approved processes; do not execute account, session, policy, or blocking changes.
Escalate confirmed compromise or high-confidence suspicious activity with supporting sign-in, MFA, audit, and downstream evidence.
review: Document what confirmed, weakened, disproved, or blocked the hypothesis, including accounts, sign-ins, risk signals, pivots, and confidence.
Record telemetry gaps, validated false positives, useful queries, and decision criteria for future hunts.
Tune detections and improve controls for the validated cloud identity behaviors.
Update identity response playbooks, reports, and this CHASER Card with lessons learned.

splunk_spl: index=cloud_identity (risk_level=high OR risk_state=atRisk OR action=failure) | stats count by user, src_ip, app, country
index=cloud_audit (operation="Add member to role" OR operation="Consent to application" OR operation="New-InboxRule") | stats count by user, operation, src_ip
sentinel_kql: SigninLogs | where RiskLevelDuringSignIn in ("medium","high") or ResultType != 0 | project TimeGenerated, UserPrincipalName, IPAddress, Location, AppDisplayName, RiskLevelDuringSignIn, ResultType
AuditLogs | where OperationName has_any ("Add member to role","Consent to application","Add service principal","New-InboxRule") | project TimeGenerated, InitiatedBy, OperationName, TargetResources
elastic_lucene: event.category:authentication AND (event.outcome:failure OR risk.level:(medium OR high))

safety_constraints.privacy_note: Use workspace privacy settings. Do not include secrets in prompts, logs, reports, or debug output.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK T1078.004: Valid Accounts - Cloud Accounts
MITRE ATT&CK DET0560: Detection of Valid Account Abuse
Microsoft Learn: Investigate Risk with Microsoft Entra ID Protection
